title: CEA Economic Report of the President 2026 - Inflation and the CPI
content: Chapter 14 of the 2026 Economic Report of the President discusses inflation as measured by the headline consumer price index and reports the data published by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The CEA distinguishes the all-items, or headline, CPI from core CPI, which excludes food and energy inflation. Core inflation is reported separately because food and energy prices tend to swing more sharply and more frequently than the rest of the consumer basket, so the core measure captures the trend component of inflation that is most relevant for monetary policy. Within the core CPI, the Report further separates core services inflation from core goods inflation. The services component captures persistent wage-linked price pressures, while the goods component is more sensitive to global supply chains, trade policy, and the commodity cycle. The Report's framing implies that inflation is read on a 12-month percent-change basis, that headline and core are tracked side by side, and that a divergence between the core services and core goods components is informative about whether inflation is being driven by domestic demand, supply-side factors, or both. The CEA interprets sustained inflation near the Federal Reserve's target as consistent with macroeconomic stability. Gray bars in the Report's figures indicate NBER-dated recessions, so the inflation series is routinely overlaid on the business-cycle chronology. Analysts following the CPIAUCSL series can apply this same framework: compare all-items to core, split core into goods and services, read twelve-month change against the target, and note whether current prints fall inside or outside recessionary episodes dated by NBER.
